title: St. Melitios of Antioch, St. Polyeuctus, St. Isaac of Nineveh & St. Leo the Great
narrative:
St. Melitios of Antioch, St. Polyeuctus the Martyr, St. Isaac of Nineveh, and St. Leo the Great of Rome

On the eighteenth of Yekatit, the holy Church places before the faithful four figures whose lives encompass the full arc of Christian witness from the first generation of martyrs through the great theologians of the patristic age to the mystical solitaries of the Syrian tradition. Saint Melitios of Antioch, who presided at the Council of Constantinople in 381 and died in the midst of its deliberations, is the anchor of this assembly: a man of such evident holiness that the theologians of opposing parties — Nicene and semi-Arian — both claimed him as their own. The tradition records that when Melitios first appeared in Antioch to take up his see, the whole city came out to see him, and those who were too far away to hear his voice were satisfied simply to receive his blessing from a distance. His personal sanctity accomplished what his theology could not have accomplished alone: it created, for a brief season, a space in which divided Christians remembered their unity.

Saint Polyeuctus the Martyr is the soldier-saint of Armenia whose story the Ethiopian Synaxarium preserves as one of the most dramatically concentrated accounts of the grace of conversion. Polyeuctus was a Roman officer at Melitene in Armenia whose friend Nearchus was a Christian; when the persecution was proclaimed, Nearchus mourned that he must lose his dearest companion, and Polyeuctus comforted him by declaring that he too would become a Christian. He had a dream that night in which Christ clothed him in radiant armour, and the next morning he went to the public square, seized the imperial edict ordering persecution, tore it in pieces, and smashed the idols being carried in procession. He was arrested and executed before he could be formally baptised, and the tradition is unanimous that he was baptised in his own blood.

Saint Isaac of Nineveh — Isaac the Syrian — is one of the greatest mystical theologians in the history of Christianity: a bishop who resigned his see after only five months because he recognised that he lacked the temperament for ecclesiastical governance, and who spent the rest of his long life in a cave near the monastery of Rabban Shabur, composing the extraordinary discourses on prayer, compunction, and divine mercy that have nourished the contemplative tradition of every Eastern church for fourteen centuries. Isaac taught that the love of God is so boundless that in the end it will embrace even those in Gehenna, and that a heart that has truly entered into prayer cannot but be moved with compassion for every living thing, including the demons. His theology of divine mercy makes him a fitting companion on the day that follows the Feast of Kidane Meheret.

Saint Leo the Great of Rome brings this assembly to its conclusion with the voice of the Western patriarchate at its most authoritative and most pastoral. His Tome, sent to the Council of Chalcedon, shaped the Christological formula of that council, and his sermons — on the Nativity, on the Passion, on the Ascension — are masterpieces of Latin theological prose that have been read in the Western church at the liturgical hours since his own time. The Ethiopian Church, which did not receive Chalcedon, nonetheless recognises in Leo a pastor of extraordinary gifts, and his commemoration in the Synaxarium alongside Isaac of Nineveh is itself a kind of ecumenical act: the mystic of the East and the administrator of the West, the contemplative and the preacher, equally servants of the one Lord whose mystery neither the council chamber nor the cave can fully contain. Amen.
